title: The Words of the Wise
summary: Proverbs 22-24 closes the main Solomonic collection and opens a new section, the Sayings of the Wise, whose striking parallels with the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope show Israel absorbing and reframing international wisdom under the fear of the LORD.
context:
Today's reading crosses a visible seam in the book. The long run of one-line Solomonic proverbs comes to a close, and at 22:17 a fresh voice begins: Incline your ear, and hear the words of the wise. This unit, the Thirty Sayings of the Wise, runs through 24:22, followed by a short appendix of further sayings in 24:23-34. The form shifts too, from clipped couplets to longer instructions with reasons attached.

This is also the passage where the Bible most clearly converses with the broader ancient Near East. Since Adolf Erman's work in 1924, scholars have recognized that nearly every saying in Proverbs 22:17-23:11 has a parallel in the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope. The Hebrew text even seems to count itself, calling its contents thirty sayings, matching Amenemope's thirty numbered chapters.

Yet this is adaptation, not copying. The Hebrew sages reorder the material, swap Egyptian images for local ones, add lines with no Egyptian source, and above all reframe the whole under the LORD as judge and refuge. International wisdom is welcomed, then resettled inside Israel's covenant faith.
themes:
Wisdom that crosses borders. The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, yet wisdom itself is not confined to Israel. These chapters show God's people learning from Egypt's best teachers and then baptizing what they learned into their own worship. Truth recognized anywhere can be received gratefully and reordered under God.

Formation begins early. Start children off on the way they should go, and even when they are old they will not turn from it. This is the language of direction and habit, of shaping a life before it sets. It is a probability, not a guarantee, but it presses the long, patient work of training the young.

Money, debt, and the limits of striving. The rich rule over the poor, and the borrower is slave to the lender. Do not wear yourself out to get rich. The sayings see clearly how wealth concentrates power and how anxious striving exhausts the soul, and they refuse to make prosperity the measure of a good life.

Resilience and the moment of testing. Though the righteous fall seven times, they rise again, while if you falter in a time of trouble, how small is your strength. Wisdom is not the absence of failure but the capacity to get up, and trouble is the test that reveals what strength was really there.
takeaways: Wisdom can be learned from anyone, but it is only safe when it is reordered under the fear of the LORD; receive truth widely and resettle it in faith.
Steady formation of the young, careful handling of money, and refusal of anxious striving are not minor virtues but the texture of a wise, durable life.
Righteousness is measured less by never falling than by rising again, and the time of trouble is precisely where real strength is tested.
connections:
These chapters close the great Solomonic collection read across the previous week and open a section in conversation with the wider world. The command to start children off on the way they should go echoes Deuteronomy's charge to teach God's words diligently to your children, and it is taken up again in the New Testament call to bring up children in the instruction of the Lord. The concern for debt and the poor stands in the same stream as the Law's release of debts and its protections for the vulnerable in Deuteronomy and Leviticus.

The Amenemope parallel reaches back to Egypt, Israel's formative backdrop from Genesis and Exodus, now met not as the house of slavery but as a source of wisdom to be reframed under the LORD. The summons to do justice and love the poor resonates with Micah's call to act justly and love mercy. The treatment of enemies, refusing to gloat when they fall, anticipates Proverbs 25 and Paul's instruction not to repay evil for evil. And the promise that the righteous rise again pairs with the encouragement not to grow weary in doing good.
reflection:
Where in your life are you wearing yourself out to get rich, or quietly measuring your worth by what you own? The sages would gently turn your eyes elsewhere.

And when you have fallen, whether seven times or seventy, do you believe you can rise again? The wise life is not flawless. It is the life that keeps getting up, and that learns, even from unexpected teachers, to walk in the fear of the LORD.
